data.title: Apple: As Earnings Near, Valuation And Industry Weakness Should Be Top Of Mind
data.description:
The last few months have been a really good time for shareholders of technology behemoth Apple (AAPL). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate back in August of last year, the stock has risen by 15.1%. That's more than double the 7.2% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. This move up comes on the back of strong financial performance, with revenue, profits, and cash flows, all rising nicely.

Unfortunately, the business is not exactly cheap. Even when you consider the cash cow that it is and the economic moat that it has built for itself over the last several decades, the stock is lofty. I wouldn't go so far as to say that shares are overvalued. But they are getting to a point where a more bearish assessment might not be crazy. This is especially true given broader economic concerns and a projected decline in smartphone shipments for the industry this year.

For now, I'm giving Apple some wiggle room. The company certainly deserves it considering its financial track record, industry-leading position, and robust balance sheet. But the picture can change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, after the market closes on January 29th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year.

Leading up to that point, analysts seem very optimistic, with revenue and profits expected to rise nicely. Considering how the economy looks, however, I do think investors should be cautious. Not to the point of justifying a bearish outlook. But to the point of being vigilant.

The picture looks healthy right now

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I would say that Apple is healthy right now. To see what I mean, we need only look at results covering the most recent quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, revenue amounted to $102.47 billion. That's an increase of 7.9% over the $94.93 billion the company reported a year earlier. This growth can be attributed to a couple of different items. But before we get to that, I think touching on some other metrics is important.

Profitability is a great example here. Net income for the business ballooned from $14.74 billion to $27.47 billion. This was in spite of the fact that, throughout the year, the company has faced some margin compression issues when it comes to the products that it sells. Instead, services profit margins are growing. This is entirely what you would expect. The fact of the matter is that, historically speaking, Apple has been a product-oriented firm. But various services that it provides, such as revenue generated from the App Store, its payment processing services, certain subscription services, and the like, are all high margin and grow as the company scales.

The way I have come to think about the company was not original. Back when Warren Buffett first bought shares of the company years ago, his view was that it is a consumer products company as opposed to a technology company. The company has amazing customer loyalty. And its ecosystem is where the real long term value is. The fact of the matter is that once you have Apple products, you become entrenched in its ecosystem. And migrating away from that is costly and time consuming. It also typically involves a learning curve. So at the end of the day, you are buying a consumer goods business with an immensely valuable digital ecosystem as the real value proposition.

The gross profit margin for the services part of the company grew from 74.03% in the final quarter of 2024 to 75.28% the same time of 2025. And for the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, the profit margin of 75.41% comfortably exceeded the 73.88% reported a year earlier. That doesn't sound like much of a difference. But when applied to the services revenue generated for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, this would mean an increase in pre-tax profits of $1.67 billion alone. Obviously, the rise in revenue certainly helped the business also.

Other profitability metrics for the company grew during this time. Operating cash flow expanded from $26.81 billion to $29.73 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get a surge from $20.20 billion to $34.54 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $32.50 billion to $35.55 billion. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows, all moved soundly higher. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which contracted some. But the adjusted figure for this is far more important than the GAAP figure is.

To really understand what's going on here, we need to dig a bit deeper. And this is where we get to some of the exciting data when it comes to revenue. To start with, we should probably touch on the different sales categories that the company reports. Most notably would be its famous iPhone product. In fact, during the final quarter of 2025, the iPhone was responsible for $49.03 billion of revenue for the business. That translates to 47.8% of overall sales. The revenue that the company saw here ended up being 6.1% above what was experienced the same quarter of 2024. And for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, the $209.59 billion that the company reported exceeded the $201.18 billion reported in 2024. This is not surprising to me. According to the IDC, the number of Apple smartphones sold globally in 2025 amounted to 247 million. That's out of a total of 1.25 billion smartphones sold across the planet. While total smartphone units sold increased 1.5%, the iPhone reported a 6.1% rise, with the famous iPhone Series 17 leading the charge.

There were other areas of growth that the company experienced also. For instance, Mac sales grew 12.7% year over year from $7.74 billion in the final quarter of 2024 to $8.73 billion in the final quarter of 2025. iPad sales were essentially flat at $6.95 billion. And wearables, home, and accessories revenue dipped ever so slightly from $9.04 billion to $9.01 billion. As I mentioned already, Apple has benefited from a strong and growing services category as well. The revenue increased there was 15.1%, climbing from $24.97 billion to $28.75 billion. For the year as a whole, the $109.16 billion reported for services revenue comfortably exceeded the $96.17 billion reported a year earlier. And according to management, this was driven by higher sales from advertising, as well as growth associated with cloud services and the App Store. Ultimately what's happening here is that those who acquire iPhones are creating a loop effect for revenue and, in turn, profits.

This can be very profitable. This is especially the case when talking about the services side of the business. Even though it accounted for only 28.06% of revenue during the most recent quarter, the 75.28% gross profit margin it achieved meant that 44.77% of all of Apple’s segment gross profits came from these particular operations. That's up from 42.13% a year earlier. And the fact that services revenue continues to outpace other sources of revenue in terms of growth makes this even sweeter.

We can also look at the picture through the lens of geographic sales. Management provides a good amount of data here. During the final quarter of 2025, $44.19 billion of revenue came from the Americas. That's up from the $41.66 billion reported to the previous year. Management cited higher revenue associated with both the iPhone and the services part of the business as the primary drivers here. But the company achieved growth in other markets also. Unfortunately, Greater China was not one of these, with revenue pulling back from $15.03 billion to $14.49 billion. In a previous article, I talked about how competitive the smartphone market was there. Unfortunately, that competition is in full swing. But with revenue growing nicely in other markets like Europe, Japan, and other parts of the world, I'm not worried.

If we use the 2025 and 2024 fiscal year data as an example, we can see in the chart above how shares of Apple are currently priced. These are, unfortunately, not inexpensive shares. I never like seeing a company trade at levels like this. And normally, this would elicit a bearish assessment from me. But I do provide additional wiggle room when it comes to high quality industry leaders that generate significant cash flows. And if Apple doesn't fit that description, I'm afraid nothing does. However, this high valuation does pose some risk. As I have detailed in other articles like here and here, I believe that broader economic conditions are problematic. I firmly believe the US is heading toward a recession while China is slowing down. Furthermore, even for those parties that don't expect these headwinds, the expectation is that, at least when it comes to the iPhone, the company could face challenges in 2026.

The IDC, for instance, believes that overall global smartphone shipments this year will be 0.9% lower than they were in 2025. This can be attributed to component shortages such as those involving memory they have constrained supply and pushed costs up. I would venture to say that makers of Android devices will be more challenged because they are more price-sensitive. But when it comes to Apple, an issue that was identified by the IDC is that the company's decision to delay its next base iPhone model to early 2027 will push iOS shipments down by more than 4%.

In the near term, weakness can always prove to be a problem. I don't mean that in the existential sense. Rather, I mean from a market psychology standpoint. Expensive shares do tend to drop disproportionately when times get tough. But the good news is that, in addition to being a resilient business that should thrive in the long run, Apple also has a rock solid balance sheet. The chart above, you can see that the net debt picture of the business has changed in recent years. Net debt has dropped, though its overall net debt position has gotten a bit worse if we include in that mix long term marketable securities that currently stand at $77.72 billion. Still, not very many companies have a balance sheet this healthy.

Whatever pain that we see, investors should expect management to continue buying back stock and paying capital to shareholders in the form of dividends. In the chart above, you can see that, for 2025 in its entirety, the company allocated $15.42 billion to shareholders in the form of dividends. That's up from the $15.23 billion reported a year earlier. But the real show involves share buybacks. Management spent $90.71 billion on repurchasing stock during the year. That amounted to 402 million shares and proved instrumental in bringing the company's total share count down by 5.1% from the end of 2023 through the end of 2025. These buybacks were smaller than the 499 million units that the company repurchased for $94.95 billion in 2024. But still, we are looking at a lot of capital here returning to investors.

If there is going to be any sort of weakness, we should expect it to start showing up before too long. In the table above, you can see expectations that they have for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. These results are going to be announced by management after the market closes on January 29th. The current expectation is her revenue of $138.20 billion, which would be up nicely from the $124.30 billion reported the previous year. Similarly, earnings per share are forecast to decline from $2.40 to $2.67, which would take net profits up from $36.33 billion to $40.06 billion. We unfortunately don't have estimates when it comes to other profitability metrics. But in that table, you can see robust cash flows for the first quarter of last year. If revenue and profits do in fact increase, we should expect those to grow also.

Takeaway

Taken together, I firmly believe that Apple is a high quality business. It is an industry leader and has earned its market capitalization of $3.88 trillion that it currently has. The stock is more expensive than I would like, and that is the only reason why I am taking a more neutral stance than I am a bullish one. In fact, in the event that we do see weakness for the company that is unexpected, we very well could see a decline in price. But as a long term, value oriented investor, I'm not going to worry about that until the data comes in.

data.title: PayPal: A Dirt Cheap Nexus Of Catalysts
data.description:
Even though PayPal (PYPL) did quite well over last two quarters in terms of positive earnings surprises, investors remain very cautious about this company's growth prospects. The stock is down by 18% since my previous bullish thesis, while recent EPS revisions were just slightly negative. It means that PYPL's multiples compressed further, making it even more attractively valued than it was. As a company with its core business involved in digital payments, PayPal's growth prospects directly depend on the broader economy's strength. And the outlook for the U.S. economy looks positive, meaning that PYPL is likely to continue delivering revenue growth. Besides digital payments, the company is also busy on expanding its business mix. At the same time, the management is also committed to improving cost efficiency, meaning that there will likely be multiple catalysts to trigger EPS growth in 2026. All in all, I reiterate "Strong Buy" for PYPL.

Recent developments

PayPal released its latest quarterly earnings in late October, delivering dual earnings beat. I believe that PYPL's Q3 earnings release was strong not only because it was another confident dual-beat quarter, but due to the accelerated revenue dynamic. The Q3 7.3% YoY growth was by far the best quarter in terms of revenue growth since FQ2 2024. Moreover, the quarter was marked with a notable EPS increase, from $1.20 to $1.34. The adjusted EPS growth was of high quality as it was due to the expanded operating margin, from 18.2% to 19.2%. Finally, the management raised transaction margin and EPS guidance for FY2025. With all that being said, PayPal's Q3 earnings release was definitely strong.

The company's upcoming earnings release is scheduled for February 3, not very far from now. Wall Street analysts expect Q4 revenue to be $8.8 billion, which will be 5.2% higher on a YoY basis. Surely, topline growth deceleration compared to Q3 is not the best outcome. However, PayPal is not a startup or an aggressive growth company. It pays dividends, so to me it is kind of a mix of growth and value. Thus, EPS growth prevails over revenue growth here, in my opinion. And everything looks good from the forecasted EPS growth perspective as it is expected to notably outpace revenue with an 8.2% YoY increase. The slight warning sign is that there were more negative EPS revisions from Wall Street analysts than positive ones, however the gap between number of upgrades and downgrades does not look dramatic. Moreover, the company has been delivering double-digit positive quarterly EPS surprises quite frequently since the new management took over in late 2023. Therefore, PayPal's actual Q4 EPS growth might be even more aggressive than projected by Wall Street analysts.

I am confident that PayPal has potential to deliver new positive surprises and guidance upgrades because the fundamental setup looks quite bullish. The company's core business is digital payments, and it is mostly concentrated domestically, meaning that the addressable market is expanding if the U.S. economy is growing. There were several positive signals from the macro side recently. First of all, the U.S. economy grew much faster than expected in Q3 2025 and accelerated consumer spending was one of the reasons behind this positive surprise. As a result of this surprise, numerous reputable sources recently expressed quite optimistic outlooks for the U.S. economy in 2026. For example, analysts from Vanguard expect the country's GDP growth to remain above 2% in 2026 with a quite low unemployment rate. Moreover, there was a slight upgrade from the European Central Bank regarding the euro area's economic growth in 2026. This is likely to be a tailwind for PayPal's international business.

Besides macro tailwinds, there is also a bunch of moves from the management to expand PayPal's footprint to make the business less vulnerable to setbacks in the macro environment. The company continues adding various new products and features to its ecosystem of financial services. The newest one is a partnership with the human capital management firm Paychex (PAYX) to deliver early paydays for employees. This is a solid move because Paychex has 800,000 B2B customers, meaning that the number of individuals covered is likely in millions. PayPal also expanded its reach by a few million people after launching an AI-powered shopping assistant in partnership with Perplexity which has approximately 30 million monthly active users. The mix of positive macro tailwinds and the management's endeavors to widen the audience are likely to maximize PayPal's revenue growth potential over the long-term.

Since profitability equation depends on cost efficiency as well, I also think it is vital to mention that the management reiterated their commitment to keep costs under control during the Q3 earnings call. My personal opinion that PayPal has room to make its cost structure leaner in the AI era because as a financial services company, back-office roles likely represent a significant portion of its overall 20k+ headcount. A recent report from McKinsey says that managers in banks spend significant portion of their working hours on routine compliance and documentation tasks that can be automated using AI agents. Therefore, there is a solid potential EPS upside from the costs side of the equation as well.

Valuation update

PYPL's multiples look really attractive, especially when compared to the company's last five years' averages. Comparing PYPL's current multiples to historical averages rather than the sector median looks more reasonable because of its unmatched scale and reach among fintech companies of the U.S. Moreover, PayPal also demonstrates unmatched return on equity and total capital employed compared to the sector.

Simulating a DCF model is a good choice to cross-check takeaways from ratios analysis. I am using cost of equity as a discount rate, as I usually do since I want to calculate the company's fair value from equity holders' perspective. The cost of equity is 10.52% for PayPal, according to the CAPM approach implemented below. By the way, equity risk premiums were updated recently by Aswath Damodaran, and for U.S. it currently stands at 4.46%.

The base year FCF assumption is $6.5 billion, which is the midpoint of the management's guidance. Since my thesis is bullish while the market's sentiment on PayPal is cautious, I am using ultra-conservative growth assumptions for my DCF model. Therefore, I incorporated just a 5% long-term FCF cover and a very modest 2% perpetual growth rate.

Despite using very conservative growth assumptions, PayPal's fair value as a company is significantly higher than the current market cap. Given various positive catalysts for sustained EPS growth, a 59% discount doesn't look fair.

Risks update

While I am bullish and have high conviction that PayPal's share price should become much higher over the long-term, I have to admit that sentiment around the stock remains extremely weak. The stock is down by 33% over the last twelve months, while the broader market demonstrated double-digit growth. The fact that a few reputable Wall Street analysts [Morgan Stanley, BofA Securities, JPMorgan] expressed cautious opinions on PayPal recently is also not very good for the sentiment. Therefore, we might continue witnessing sentiment-driven share price weakness for a while.

The U.S. economy looks strong, but it doesn't mean that there are no potential adverse factors on the horizon. Geopolitical situation has got even more complicated within just two first weeks of 2026 after the U.S. military operation in Venezuela and a potential one in Iran. Moreover, recently revealed information that the Fed Chairman, Jerome Powell, is under criminal investigation is widely viewed as unprecedented and might make investors quite nervous. This year's midterm elections add another layer of political uncertainty, which can weigh on investors' sentiment. And if the broader U.S. equity market declines, it is difficult to imagine that PYPL will move in the opposite direction. Therefore, even if PYPL's fundamentals continue improving, its share price reaction might be muted if sentiment around the broader market deteriorates.

Bottom line

To conclude, I think that PYPL remains a "Strong Buy". I think that the recent share price weakness was sentiment-driven because there are several positive catalysts around the company. Moreover, valuation is too cheap to miss out this compelling opportunity.

data.title: Veracyte: Profitable, De-Risked, And Ready To Run
data.description:
My bullish view on Veracyte (VCYT) is not simply based upon my hopes for the future of the company's technologies, but upon the evidence of the company's current financial performance. To date, Veracyte has successfully transitioned out of the speculative phase of its corporate life cycle, and has established itself as a diversified, profitable business model with significant operating leverage. Veracyte is entering the year with three major catalysts: increased penetration in prostate cancer, a synergy rich entry into the minimal residual disease market, and the re-launch of their breast cancer product in the U.S., all of which will be supported by a balance sheet that is now self-fortifying.

Latest Financials

Veracyte anticipates total revenue of between $515 million and $517 million for FY 2025, up 16% over the prior year. A lot can be said about the top-line growth in terms of revenue, but the way the revenue is composed is equally as important. The testing revenue, which is the key component of the high-margin recurring revenue of the company, is anticipated to come in between $491 million and $493 million.

The volume data further indicate a strong demand for Veracyte's services. The company anticipates reporting total testing volume of approximately 169,700 for the year, representing a 19% increase in volume. The Decipher prostate franchise, leading the charge in terms of volume, saw volumes grow 27% to approximately 102,000 tests. Even the mature Afirma thyroid business was able to post an 11% volume increase to approximately 67,700 tests, indicating that it is far from being a stagnant legacy asset.

However, the most telling aspects of the financial update were not the revenue numbers, but the profitability profile of Veracyte. Veracyte anticipates reporting an adjusted EBITDA margin of greater than 25% for the full year 2025. It did not happen by accident, but was due to the strict cost discipline and inherent leverage of their platform. For the 12-month period ended December 31, 2022, the company had reported a GAAP net loss of $36.5 million. Jump ahead to the end of 2024 and the net loss had flipped to a positive GAAP net income of $24.1 million. Although the GAAP net income number for 2025 has not yet been finalized, the trend indicates a company that has fundamentally changed the unit economics of their business model.

A Phased Approach to Growth

A major upcoming growth driver is expected to be the TrueMRD for Muscle Invasive Bladder Cancer, launching in the first half of 2026. Entering the minimal residual disease space represents a significant strategic development opportunity for Veracyte, representing a new total addressable market. However, the entry into this new market will leverage their existing strength as urologists and radiation oncologists who currently purchase the Decipher Prostate test for prostate cancer are largely the same clinicians managing bladder cancer. Therefore, Veracyte can simply place a high value recurrence monitoring product into an already warm sales channel. This is the definition of commercial synergy. For example, if a start-up were to create a bladder test it would have to hire a sales force from scratch. On the other hand, Veracyte can simply provide their existing reps with a new product to carry in their bag, creating immediate operating leverage.

Another is the Prosigna breast cancer test, anticipated to be launched as a Laboratory Developed Test in the United States in mid-2026. The company is moving forward with this transition after the positive outcome data from the 10-year OPTIMA PRELIM study was presented in May 2025. While Veracyte is transitioning to an LDT model in the U.S., the company continues to pursue IVD certification in Europe. The breast cancer market is large and highly competitive, however, having a proprietary product that provides deep biological insights into recurrence risk enables Veracyte to compete based on the quality of the information provided rather than price.

Veracyte has also completed enrollment in the NIGHTINGALE pivotal trial, evaluating the clinical utility of the Percepta Nasal Swab for the assessment of lung cancer risk. A successful readout could potentially open up a huge population of patients with incidental lung nodules who are now undergoing unnecessary invasive procedures. The ability to rule out cancer by using a simple nasal swab would be a major paradigm shift in pulmonology, similar to what Afirma accomplished for thyroid nodules years ago.

Data Moats and Digital Pathology

I believe there are many unappreciated assets at Veracyte beyond the basic measures of revenue and volume. The company's strategic updates highlighted the creation of a large digital pathology database by scanning all slides. The database currently contains over 175,000 slides from over 130,000 de-identified patients, which are each associated with long-term outcome data.

With the advent of artificial intelligence, this massive biorepository represents a highly valuable and intangible asset. A number of start-ups in the healthcare AI space have developed sophisticated algorithms but lack the quality and quantity of ground truth data (high-quality images with corresponding patient outcomes) needed to properly train these algorithms. For example, an AI may be able to generate an image, but without the longitudinal patient outcomes, the AI model lacks a meaningful framework to use in developing its predictions.

Veracyte, however, has both the genomic data generated from their assays and the digital pathology images linked together with corresponding patient outcomes. Together, the genomic data and digital pathology images represent a powerful competitive barrier to entry. Developing a similar database as Veracyte would require a significant investment of time and resources, likely taking a competitor more than a decade to develop a database of similar size and scope.

The development of this database also provides Veracyte with a potential pathway to new digital-only products. For example, one can imagine a future where Veracyte develops a rapid, AI-based risk assessment product using only the digitized pathology slide, providing a low-cost funnel for Veracyte’s more expensive genomic tests. The development of the digital pathology initiative also allows Veracyte to evolve from a simple testing laboratory to a bioinformatics platform. As Veracyte continues to add to the database with each test performed, their AI models will continually improve and provide more accurate and predictive results, ultimately allowing Veracyte to identify new biomarkers or purely digital signatures that possess high clinical value with no incremental costs of goods sold. Ultimately, the conversion to a bioinformatics platform may allow Veracyte to eventually earn a valuation multiple more commensurate with those of other health-tech companies rather than traditional diagnostic laboratories.

Counterarguments and Risks

A major risk to the company's valuation lies in the complexity of the European market. Veracyte has chosen a decentralized strategy in Europe by marketing their tests as in vitro diagnostic kits that can be run on local instrumentation such as the nCounter or qPCR systems. Although this may avoid some of the logistics associated with shipping samples to the U.S., the regulatory environment in Europe under the new IVDR framework is extremely complex and slow. Delays in receiving IVDR certification for their future pipeline products could delay their international revenue growth and create over-reliance on the U.S. market.

Additionally, entering the minimal residual disease market with TrueMRD positions Veracyte as a competitor to large established players. The MRD space is crowded and capital intensive. While Veracyte's whole genome approach - using just one tube of blood - is differentiated and potentially more sensitive than competitors, clinical adoption can be difficult. Oncologists who have ordered from a competitor may resist changing their workflow to accommodate Veracyte. In order for Veracyte to successfully enter the MRD space, they will need to demonstrate not only that their product works, but also how it integrates seamlessly into clinical practice and how it provides sufficient value to displace the existing workflow. Slow adoption of TrueMRD in bladder cancer would diminish a key growth driver for Veracyte through the middle term.

TAM and Competitors

Veracyte has room to grow, even in the U.S. prostate cancer market, where the company estimates there are approximately 334,000 patients per year in the United States who are diagnosed with prostate cancer. Veracyte currently claims Decipher holds 33% of the total available market in the U.S. prostate cancer space. This gives Veracyte a strong leadership position within the prostate cancer space, however, it indicates that there may be an additional two thirds of the available prostate cancer space that is still available. This share of the prostate cancer space includes both competitors utilizing older nomograms and genomic classifier-based approaches, as well as a large percentage of physicians that have yet to use genomic testing in this space. Herein lies the opportunity - while there will certainly be opportunities to steal share, there is also the opportunity to grow the overall size of the market by converting those physicians into users of genomic testing.

The breast cancer space represents a much larger, but far more competitive landscape than either the prostate cancer space or the thyroid cancer space. Within the U.S., the company estimates that there are approximately 225,000 eligible patients who could utilize Prosigna. However, this number does represent the largest challenge for Veracyte, as it faces intense competition from Exact Sciences (EXAS) with their Oncotype DX product. I believe that Veracyte's approach of differentiating their Prosigna product through the provision of recurrence risk and biological classification data is sound; however, I anticipate that Veracyte will face significant challenges in achieving meaningful market share in the U.S. breast cancer space relative to their success in urology and endocrinology.

Future Outlook

I believe Veracyte presents a promising opportunity by providing the growth of an earlier-stage biotech company along with the financial discipline of a mature company. The preliminary results from 2025 will be used as a proof point to demonstrate that Veracyte can grow revenue at the same time as increasing margin levels. Management's forecast of revenue for 2026, ranging from $570 million to $582 million with a constant 25% Adjusted EBITDA margin, demonstrates that the business model has entered into a phase of consistent and profitable execution. The turnaround is complete and the platform is in place, and now, I think Veracyte is positioned to reap the benefits of its operational discipline.

data.title: NBXG: Flawed Portfolio Structure Limits Appeal (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As market indices continue to trade near all-time highs, it can be difficult to locate discounted opportunities. However, many income-focused funds continue to trade at discounted valuations, and this may present an opportunity for long-term investors. For instance, the Neuberger Next-Generation Connectivity Fund (NBXG) continues to trade at a deep discount to NAV valuation. When I previously covered the fund, I issued a buy rating due to the strong portfolio strategy and attractive valuation. Since my last coverage, NBXG has released an updated annual report that I wanted to assess now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NBXG's share price has increased by about 13.6%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last twelve months. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to nearly 25.7% over the same time frame. NBXG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 9.8%. The fund issues payouts on a monthly basis, and I believe the payouts are well supported by earnings at this time. Therefore, NBXG remains a great option for income investors seeking a reliable stream of dividend income.

While the reporting indicates that NBXG's dividend is well supported, there are some vulnerabilities that stand out to me. For instance, the fund is heavily reliant on positive market momentum since essentially all of its growth comes from net realized gains. Additionally, the fund continues to underperform some notable peers out there, despite having direct equity exposure to some of the highest-quality growth companies. Lastly, the fund offers almost no resilience against market declines, and this can threaten the health of the dividend. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy that NBXG implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

NBXG has total managed assets of $1.3B that are spread across a diverse range of companies that are involved with the next generation of mobile network connectivity and technology. The fund aims to provide attractive total returns that are achieved through the combination of both capital appreciation and income generation. In order to achieve this goal, the fund is primarily invested in public equities. According to the latest fund snapshot, the portfolio has exposure to 59 public equities and 11 restricted investments.

Looking at the portfolio's breakdown, we can see that it is most heavily allocated to large-cap companies. Since NBXG is focused on the innovative drivers in the mobile and network connectivity sector, we can see that the fund is most heavily allocated to information technology companies at 42.2% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to communication services and consumer discretionary investments, which account for 20.9% and 12% of assets, respectively.

However, NBXG does not utilize any sort of debt securities as part of its portfolio strategy. Therefore, the fund implements an option writing strategy against a portion of its underlying holdings to help generate amplified income levels. The latest snapshot confirms that 22.8% of the fund's portfolio on an average daily basis was overwritten. This implies that the remaining 77.2% of assets are able to fully participate in the growth of the market. Therefore, the option writing strategy is a relatively small piece of the fund's total assets and portfolio strategy. Although this is a smaller piece of the strategy, the inclusion of written options can still limit upside growth potential over time.

I really like the fund's transparency when it comes to its holdings because NBXG provides some insight into the private entities. However, we can see that the weight of private/restricted holdings makes up a relatively small portion of its total assets. When looking at the top ten public holdings, we can see that Robinhood Markets (HOOD) is the largest position at 5.42% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable names, including Meta Platforms (META), Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), just to name a few.

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, NBXG traded at a discount to NAV of 11.09%. However, the price has declined a bit since then, so the fund now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 11.49%. For reference, NBXG has traded at an average discount to NAV of 13.97% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can also see that NBXG's price-to-NAV valuation remains below peers. However, the fund's operating history is so short that this may not provide enough helpful context around its current valuation.

Since the time of my last coverage, NBXG has released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the only report available that highlights the fund's internal performance in terms of net investment income and net realized gains. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.09 per share, while net realized gains landed at $4.17 per share. Looking back at the fund's prior years, we can see that net investment income has consistently generated losses for the fund. Since NBXG prioritizes income generation, I would love to see a future where the net investment income levels are substantially improved because this eliminates the fund's reliance on net realized gains.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market momentum. For instance, NBXG is more likely to capture meaningful net realized gains during periods of positive market growth, which enables the fund to sell positions at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. However, this also means that NBXG offers no resilience against market declines. As we can see from 2022's performance, this can lead to large net realized losses that can substantially erode the underlying NAV over time. NBXG still hasn't fully recovered from the loss experienced back in 2022. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.92 per share
  • 2022: $11.68 per share
  • 2023: $11.92 per share
  • 2024: $14.49 per share
  • 2025: $17.35 per share

Performance Risks

Despite the fund providing direct equity exposure to some of the highest-quality growth companies, I believe that investors need to limit their expectations around NBXG's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. As we've established, the fund exclusively relies on positive net realized gains to support the distributions over time. This means that NBXG must actively sell off portions of the upside growth of its holdings over time. This eliminates the fund's ability to let some of its strongest positions continue their positive momentum over time.

To demonstrate this weakness, we can refer to the fund's performance since inception. As we can see below, NBXG's share price has actually declined by 27.1% since its inception. Despite market indices hovering around all-time highs, the fund has not been able to share the same positive momentum. When including all distributions that were paid out, the fund's total return jumps up to nearly 14.9% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should expect that over 90% of their total return will come from the distributions paid when held over a longer time frame.

Additionally, NBXG's focus on income generation has led to underperformance against more traditional ETFs that focus on connectivity companies. I want to put an emphasis that an investor's decision to focus on income generation may result in severe underperformance over a longer holding period. Since NBXG has to sell off portions of its upside growth, the fund will not be able to keep up with traditional ETFs that have a more straightforward approach. I believe that two alternative ETFs that can be attractive for investors seeking exposure to the sector are the following:

  1. Defiance Connective Technologies ETF (SIXG)
  2. Invesco Next Gen Connectivity ETF (KNCT)

Despite these funds having different portfolio strategies, sector allocations, and positions, the underlying emphasis of capturing the growth of the connectivity sector remains similar. Although these three funds hold many of the same positions, NBXG's structure isn't as effective at capturing the upside growth of the sector. Even when including all distributions paid out, we can see how NBXG has underperformed these alternatives since inception. SIXG has the highest total return of more than 90% since NBXG's inception. I anticipate that NBXG's underperformance is very likely to continue over the next decade, simply because the focus on income limits gains.

Strong Dividend Coverage

According to the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.12 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.8%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.44 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that the fund has no problems generating earnings that far exceed the distributions being paid out. For this reason, I have no concerns around the overall sustainability of payouts for income investors. For reference, here are the year-end earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $1.32 per share
  • 2022: ($8.04) per share
  • 2023: $1.44 per share
  • 2024: $3.77 per share
  • 2025: $4.08 per share

What I like most about the current distribution setup is that payouts aren't overly generous. This allows the fund to produce healthy NAV growth during periods where the fund is able to capture higher earnings. Additionally, the fund has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that are strong enough to support several years worth of payouts at times. The latest example of this was demonstrated over the course of 2025:

2025's earnings of $4.08 per share / $1.44 annual distribution = 2.83 years.

So although the earnings may be variable based on market conditions, the portfolio strategy has proven to be efficient. So judging from the fund's decision to raise payouts by 20% back in September, I believe that this is sustainable in the near term. The raise can also be an indication of management's confidence that they'll be able to support the higher payouts over time.

The annual report also indicates that all the payouts for 2025 were classified as return of capital. To confirm this, I checked the latest available tax document, which also confirmed that return of capital is being used. Return of capital in this instance is beneficial because these forms of distributions are not taxed as income. Instead, return of capital distributions reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. However, I would suggest caution here since the method in which distributions are paid can shift at any time, which may lead to unexpected tax burdens for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on NBXG to a hold. Although the fund continues to trade at a discount to NAV valuation, I believe there are some structural flaws that need to be adjusted. For instance, the fund's reliance on net realized gains means that NBXG is highly vulnerable to downward swings in the market. Since net investment income performance is so poor, the fund has no way to generate income during unfavorable market conditions. So even if the fund isn't able to generate positive earnings during a year, it will still issue a large distribution. This is likely to lead to NAV erosion over time unless net investment income is significantly improved. Lastly, the fund continues to severely underperform more traditional sector-tracking ETFs, such as SIXG and KNCT.

data.title: Venture Global: Still Too Expensive To Pull The Trigger
data.description:
Venture Global (VG) is an LNG (liquefied natural gas) export company with ambitions to grow into the largest such company in the world. While the company is profitable, there are risks to this thesis that make holding the public equity of the parent company questionable even at $8 per share. I rate it a sell, but would consider buying if it completely washed out. With that as a preamble, let’s dive into the details.

Intro to LNG Exporting Companies

LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) export companies use massive, industrial-scale refrigeration systems to cool natural gas. Because the gas must reach a temperature of approximately -160°C (-260°F) to turn into a liquid, the equipment used is far more complex than a standard refrigerator. In the industry, the specific unit where this cooling happens is called a Liquefaction Train. In liquid form, natural gas is 1/600th the volume of its gaseous form. Shrinking it to that scale allows the natural gas to be loaded and transported on LNG export ships to various regasification facilities around the world, which, through a reverse process, turn that liquid back into gas and inject it into the local pipeline network.

In short, an LNG export facility allows natural gas, which is abundant and cheap in North America, to be exported to Europe, Asia, South America, and anywhere else that needs it. As we shall see, building an LNG export facility is capital intensive and requires enormous injections of cash to build. In turn, the bondholders require contractual arrangements that typically run for 20 years. Anything less than 20-year agreements makes handing over $10+ billion a skittish affair.

VG's Strategy

Venture Global has proven that they can sign enough customers to long-term contracts to satisfy bondholders and build facilities at scale (on time and reasonably on budget), and this makes them a frontrunner to become the largest LNG export company in the world. Their goal is to build facilities in Louisiana that will eventually export 100 MTPA – million tons per annum. A million tons of LNG is around 48 to 49 billion cubic feet, and 100 MTPA would be the equivalent of exporting 13.3 Bcf/d (billion cubic feet per day), which easily doubles the export capacity of the US LNG.

They currently have a self-reported export capacity of around 38 MTPA (about 30% of US export capacity) with another 28 MTPA in the construction phase for a total of 66.2 MTPA (about 8.8 Bcf/d). Behind that, they have another expansion in the construction phase – CP2 (Calcasieu Pass 2) and two more in development—CP3 and an expansion of Plaquemines. For a single company to export this much LNG is an extraordinary engineering and financial feat, and one way to accomplish that is to promise a lot of things to a lot of people, as we shall see.

The goal with commissioning each new facility, is to require less externally generated capital and to build bigger and faster than before. As noted in the chart, their recently completed Plaquemine facility is over 2 times bigger than Calcasieu Pass. Once phase 1 and 2 of CP2 are complete, it will require approximately $29B in total capital and include up to 30 MTPA of export capacity.

In addition to the LNG facilities themselves, they also own three completed pipeline header systems to feed those facilities: TransCameron, a 42 inch, 24 mile header system that feeds Calcasieu Pass, Gator Express, a 27-mile pipeline, 48 inch header system that feeds Plaquemine up to 3.9 Bcf/d of natural gas and a recently completed CP Express, a 91.4 mile, 48 inch pipeline that will feed CP2. To help feed gas into CP Express, they also co-developed and own 50% of Blackfin Pipeline, which is still in the construction phase but will be ready by the time CP2 is ready to receive feed gas. Blackfin is jointly owned by Whitewater and is a 193-mile extension of the Matterhorn Express Pipeline, which was completed in 2024. Matterhorn is owned by Whitewater and other midstream companies. Blackfin also has the potential to receive feed gas from Eiger Express, a massive 3.7 Bcf/d new pipeline that will move into the construction phase early in 2026 and is scheduled for a mid-2028 in service date.

On the waterfront, Venture Global owns 9 LNG vessels and has chartered two more, which collectively have the capacity to export 15% of their current LNG production. Most LNG exported from their facilities is chartered by their customers.

A Massive Stack of Capital

The “capital stack” for Venture Global to pay for all of this infrastructure is complex, but understanding it is key to understanding the value of owning the publicly traded stock. Here’s the published “simplified” organizational structure. One has to chuckle at the word “simplified,” because this is anything but simple.

One of the purposes of the complex structure is to define legally who or what entity can lay claim to each project’s income, and in the event of a default, which entity can lay claim to the associated assets. From the perspective of the public common equity holder, we can further simplify the structure as follows:

Starting at the top and working our way down: As of Oct 31st, 2025, there are 479.5MM shares of the publicly traded Class A common stock and 1,968.6MM shares of Class B (plus another 192.9MM shares for employees). The Class B stock is owned by insiders and other early investors, and these shares have 10x the voting power of Class A stock. The 3 million VG-LNG perpetual preferred shares have a value of $2.9B with a 9% per annum dividend payout. As a reminder, preferred shares rank senior to common shares.

The 9 million VG-CP Funding Preferred shares were part of an early $1.3B equity package that Stonepeak provided in 2019 to fund the Calcasieu Pass facility. The terms of these preferred shares are expensive with annual 1% escalators (after commissioning, which occurred earlier in 2025). Distributions are “paid-in-kind.” Paid-in-kind means that distributions are paid in additional shares rather than cash dividends. Currently this pays 11% per annum, and there are clauses that require all the available cash flow from Calcasieu to be payable to the preferred shares (to redeem all the additional distributed shares since inception) before any remaining cash flow from Calcasieu Pass can be paid to the common equity. Then in 2027, all available cash flow from Calcasieu Pass must go towards redeeming the remaining shares. I assume these shares will eventually be purchased by Venture Global using cash from operations or perhaps by issuing either debt or new preferred equity at more favorable terms.

Rounding out the equity portion, Stonepeak owns a 23% common equity stake in the Calcasieu Pass facility, with Venture Global owning the remaining 77%. The book value of the 23% share is $583MM. Stonepeak used to own a larger share of common shares in Calcasieu, but Venture Global repurchased some shares in preceding years.

Finally, there is $32.6B of debt occupying almost every layer of the company, from construction loans to build CP2, to facility level debt for Calcasieu Pass and Plaquemines, to pipeline debt for Blackfin, to $11B Senior Secured Notes of Venture Global LNG. Construction debt is running 8.8% in aggregate, and debt for finished projects is running 7.5%. The aggregated interest on all $32.6B is 8%, with interest for the construction loans being capitalized. Whew! That’s a lot of capital! Total enterprise value for Venture Global sits at $57B, with the common shares of Venture Global LNG making up 37% of that capital structure (as of Jan 13, 2026). Venture Global LNG seeks to own all or substantially all the equity ownership in its current five LNG projects and any future projects, which will simplify the structure.

How LNG Exporting Companies Get Paid

The next key piece for us to understand is how an LNG facility typically gets paid. A typical simplified sale might look something like this with costs listed per MMBtu of LNG sold:

Henry Hub is a natural gas pipeline header system located in Erath, Louisiana, and is the primary pricing benchmark for the North American natural gas market. For LNG contractual arrangements (whether short, medium, or long-term), feed gas is typically indexed to Henry Hub and delivered to the facility at “cost-plus.” The 15% markup covers the fuel use—both the fuel used to transport it to the facility and the fuel consumed in the liquefaction process. The Liquefaction Fee covers the facility’s operating and capital costs plus profit. Typically, the 20-year contractual costs for liquefaction fees range from $1.75 to $3.50 per MMBtu, depending on the contract, but they can also be sold on the spot market for higher amounts.

The Transportation and regasification costs cover the boat, fuel, tolls, docking fees, and the fees associated with regasification on the other side of the pond. In the example above, $4.86 is a very lucrative liquefaction fee, and more than covers the actual cost to liquefy natural gas and pay for the capital costs. In long-term contractual arrangements, the customer pays a much smaller liquefaction fee on a take-or-pay basis, and when the LNG is sold, they pocket the difference between the spot price for natural gas overseas and the contractual liquefaction fee. In spot market sales, the LNG exporting facility pockets the full amount—in our example, they earn the full $4.86 per MMBtu.

TTF is the Henry Hub of Europe—TTF stands for Title Transfer Facility. It is a virtual trading point for natural gas in the Netherlands and serves as the primary pricing benchmark for all of Europe. The key point to remember is that TTF (and the Asian version—JKM, Platts Japan Korea Marker) trades in line with Brent crude oil on a BTU basis, and Henry Hub trades at a substantial discount to Brent crude oil. The arbitrage between those two values is what makes LNG trading so profitable.

I suppose it’s possible that an LNG exporting facility could buy the gas for less than Henry Hub and therefore make an additional profit on the front end, but for Venture Global this turns out not to be case. At least not yet:

In fact, for the first 9 months of 2025, I calculate that their feed gas costs exceeded their cost structure by $0.23 per MMBtu, even with the 15% markup, so there is pricing risk embedded in the model. Blackfin pipeline, purchase agreements with natural gas producers in the US and becoming contractual shippers on pipelines that draw natural gas from major shale basins—these are all strategies to reduce that risk.

Since the feed gas costs are indexed to Henry Hub and the transportation and regasification fees are relatively fixed, the liquefaction fee is where the money is made, so the next key piece to understand is how liquefaction fees are determined. Venture Global’s strategy has been to price long-term contracts low to entice the rapid sign-up of customers, and they have been very successful in this endeavor.

For Calcasieu Pass, the average liquefaction fee earned in the third quarter of 2025 was $1.76/MMbtu, which is on the low side. The fourth quarter of 2025, saw this figure increase to $2.01 (due in part to a larger volume of spot cargoes sold), which is an improvement. These long-term contracts do have inflation escalators, but even so, these fixed fees seem brutally low, given the risk the facility is assuming and all the externally funded equity it raised, and the terms of its preferred equity as discussed above. If you then tack on the cost of pending arbitrations (more on this below), then this facility's cash flow could become even more burdened over time.

Calcasieu Pass is past their COD (commercial operation date), where LNG cargoes are now sold primarily on 20-year contracts with low fixed liquefaction fees. Their strategy is to sell 20-year contracts for all the nameplate capacity at these low fixed liquefaction rates and then sell the expected excess capacity at higher rates. (I believe that Plaquemine’s long-term contracts are priced closer to $2.30/MMBtu, and CP2 rates are even higher). Remember, long-term contracts satisfy the bondholders and VG’s speed in setting up facilities and acquiring funding can potentially crowd out a very competitive field of new LNG exporting facilities.

Calcasieu has 10 MTPA of nameplate capacity and 2.4 MTPA of potential excess capacity (although they only shipped about 0.2 MTPA of that excess capacity in the fourth quarter). Plaquemines has 20 MTPA of nameplate capacity and 7.2 MTPA of excess capacity (shipping around 1.5 MTPA of that excess capacity in the fourth quarter). As mentioned, Calcasieu is past its COD; Plaquemines is still commissioning and will reach the COD by the end of 2026 for phase 1 and early 2027 for phase 2. The commissioning phase allows Venture Global to sell their cargoes on the spot market and capture all the arbitrage. In the third quarter of 2025, the average fixed liquefaction fee for Plaquemine was a juicy $6.79/MMBtu, dropping to $6.02/MMBtu in the 4th quarter. Healthy profits from previous projects pay for the equity in new projects and allow them to start construction earlier. Consequently, this strategy of selling spot cargoes at rich premiums during the commissioning phase of each facility reduces the risk for investors across the board.

Putting a Price Tag on Venture Global

In terms of income and value of the common stock, we need to do some analysis, and this starts with the income statement. Net income attributable to common stockholders was $429MM in Q3 or $0.18 per undiluted share or $0.16 per diluted share. This equates to a price to earnings ratio of 12 or if you prefer to deal in percentages, it is yielding about 8%. Given that the S&P 500 is trading at a lofty 29 P/E for Q3 (as reported earnings) or 3.4%, you can comfortably place your buy order now, knowing the earnings will be substantially discounted to that. This is a reasonable placeholder, and it would make the stock a relative buy if this income had any legs. As a reminder, net income accounts for the cost of capital consumed (depreciation) and the future cost of cash income taxes, so this is a reasonable perspective. Using the diluted share count also allows for all the common stock that has to date been promised to employees.

For another view into earnings, we can look at the run rate EBITDA for Q4 2025 and note that they will earn approximately $1.94B in EBITDA (based on updated guidance that VG has provided) - annualized that’s $7.7B per year. That’s an enterprise value to EBITDA of 7.4 with anything less than 10 rated a buy. That’s the surface level analysis and maybe what is propping up the stock.

Eroding Profits

However, there are three problems with relying on these figures. The first is that these earnings are being heavily supported by the spot cargoes of Plaquemines and as stated above, these spot cargoes will become contracted at a much lower rate when Plaquemines reaches COD. The second problem concerns their spot cargoes. The prices for TTF and JKM have been falling throughout 2025 because their prices are tied to Brent and the international price of oil, and Brent has been falling because OPEC flooded the market with 2.2 million barrels per day of crude oil production in the span of 6 months. This is putting the world of crude oil in an oversupplied condition that will persist into 2026 and potentially beyond, and this oversupplied crude market has dragged down the global natural gas price with it.

The third problem is that Plaquemines and the newly commissioned Golden Pass facility (see Exxon Mobil—Better Returns Are Ahead) and other facilities are temporarily flooding the global market with natural gas. It is possible that some spot cargoes go unsold, or get sold at a discount, or further pressure the price of TTF and JKM. It is not inconceivable that the international price of natural gas temporarily decouples from the price of crude oil.

Let’s start with the forward curves for Henry Hub and TTF (as of Jan 13th) and use the future spread between those two figures to estimate the downdraft to earnings in 2026. The current 12-month Henry Hub strip is trading at $3.45/MMBtu, and TTF is trading at $9.60/MMBtu. If we add in the 15% feed gas cost and subtract the $1.21 in transportation and regassification fees, we are left with a $4.42 spot price for liquefaction based on the 12-month strip. The Q4 fixed liquefaction fee for Plaquemines was $6.02/MMBtu. The delta between those two numbers is $1.60/MMBtu on 26 MTPA or $2.2B in lost EBITDA for the full-year relative to our $7.7B annualized Q4 EBITDA figure. That puts the EBITDA estimate for 2026 based on the forward strip at $5.6B compared to a street estimate of $5.9B (with 16 analysts reporting as of Jan 13). CP2 is scheduled to begin shipping cargoes in Q3 of 2027 so that won’t affect the 2026 numbers.

Looking out to 2027, we can also note that once the COD dates are reached for Plaquemines phase 1 and 2, another $1.2B in EBITDA will be eroded as contracts move to the 20-year contractual rate for the nameplate capacity. The 2027 strip pricing for Henry Hub and TTF, puts the liquefaction fee at $3.29 or another $1.13/MMBtu less than the 2026 spread, which will slice off another $0.4B in EBITDA for the excess cargoes sold. That puts us at about $4.1B in EBITDA for Plaquemines and Calcasieu Pass and the other assets in service for 2027 plus some commissioning cargoes from CP2 if it is completed on time. Perhaps they can squeeze out a bit more from Plaquemines and Calcasieu.

The downdraft in earnings adds a lot of uncertainty to the stock price, especially as estimates are revised lower. Having said that, forward curves are not 100% predictors of future prices. TTF could rise over time (especially with geopolitical uncertainty), and Henry Hub could fall, widening the spread and alleviating some of the heartburn with these numbers.

If we look at the enterprise value side of the equation, we can note that $8.2B has been invested in CP2 and if we subtract that from $57B, we arrive at $48.5B. But then we come to the 5 pending arbitrations against Venture Global, which can add $4.8B to $5.5B in debt to pay for them. It is impossible to accurately predict the outcome (or the timing for payouts), but let’s add a $3B buffer and call the enterprise value for their current assets $51.5B excluding Blackfin and CP2. Our EV/EBITDA ratio for currently producing assets then becomes $51.5B divided by $4.1B or 12.6 (for producing assets), hardly a bargain. You could argue that more EBITDA is coming with the commissioning of CP2, but on the flip side, so is a lot more debt.

Conclusion

To sum up, we have a common stock in a company selling for about $8 per share (as of Jan 15th) with almost zero voting power and a tiny 1% dividend. It debuted at $25, but the price isn’t quite low enough to be considered a bargain. Their strategy to quickly bring inexpensive natural gas to the global market at scale for low prices is the very strategy that is undermining their margins over time. On the plus side, they are retaining a decent amount of operating income, which can help fund CP2. Perhaps over 3 to 5 years, they can slowly chip away at some of their debt and preferred equity and right the LNG ship.

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: Is Elevidys A Bust?
data.description:
It has been roughly seven months since my previous Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) article titled “Sarepta Therapeutics: Rebuilding the Thesis After Hitting Rock Bottom.” At that time, SRPT was in collapse after ELEVIDYS had become a regulatory nightmare as reports of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) patients dying following treatment. ELEVIDYS went from being viewed as a once-in-a-generation franchise treatment to potentially a safety nightmare that looked like it was destined to be pulled from the market by regulators. Although I did have my concerns, I stressed that it looked like it was only going to be non-ambulatory patients that were going to be pulled from treatment, which would hurt their peak sales estimates, but the market was dismissing the long-term value of Sarepta’s Duchenne franchise. As a result, I believed SRPT was trading at a discount and decided to add to the position in the $15 handle in early August.

Seven months later, we now see that ELEVIDYS was not withdrawn but has black-box warnings, and Sarepta will likely have to deal with payer scrutiny, as well as lingering safety concerns. However, the main concern is that we are seeing a quarter-over-quarter decrease in ELEVIDYS sales. Indeed, the company just reported that their preliminary Q4 and 2025 numbers showed nearly $900M of ELEVIDYS revenue in 2025, pulling in roughly $1.86B in total product sales. Plus, they ended 2025 with almost $1B in cash. However, the market is obviously apprehensive about the fading ELEVIDYS sales and the Q4 “miss.”

It looks as if the same tension that has dictated SRPT for the past several years is still in control of the price action. The market has made a game out of constantly repricing SRPT based on headlines, which has provided plenty of trading opportunities for investors to manage their position around earnings reports, safety headlines, and commercial updates. On the downside, it makes SRPT a laborious investment as investors struggle to maintain long-term revenue models and remain vigilant for company updates. Well, the preliminary Q4/2025 numbers report could be another opportunity as the share price goes into another tailspin as the market attempts to analyze the “miss” and how ELEVIDYS will perform in the coming quarters.

I intend to provide some background on how Sarepta has become a franchise stock and how the fate of SRPT is tied to ELEVIDYS. Then, I will take a look at the preliminary numbers and will discuss how the “miss” on earnings might have investors wondering if ELEVIDYS is a bust. In addition, I will point out some bullish points from Sarepta’s presentation at the JPMorgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference. Plus, I will revisit my leading downside risk and how I plan to manage my SRPT position as we head into 2026.

The Drug Stock

I would claim that Sarepta’s stock over the past several years has been a result of investor confidence in the resilience of the company’s revenue base and the company’s R&D prowess in rare diseases, mainly DMD. The company’s earnings have evolved with their journey to becoming a DMD franchise company to what is now a company dependent on ELEVIDYS for explosive growth.

Indeed, the ticker was trading at a premium with only a fragmented exon-skipping DMD market, which was essentially overlaid by the dawdling, incremental growth of their PMO drugs. That dynamic created a rough environment for SRPT that would rally on positive clinical updates, but the gains would often fade as the Street questioned how large the business was really growing from the PMO therapies. ELEVIDYS changed that with early commercial uptake smashing through the company’s previous projected ceiling. ELEVIDYS was expected to be an option for most DMD patients, which would model a multibillion-dollar opportunity. That opportunity made SRPT transition from a DMD franchise stock to essentially an ELEVIDYS stock.

The volatility since then has simply reflected the market’s attempt to price that future correctly. So, when data, guidance, or commercial updates are positive, the stock moves substantially higher, but when safety concerns, manufacturing questions, or reimbursement issues pop up, or when sales start to slip, the stock drops. However, I must reiterate, the swings are not about whether Sarepta survives; they are more about the market’s prediction of ELEVIDYS.

Concerning Trends

Digging into Sarepta’s reported preliminary numbers, their full-year product revenue was $1.86B, with ELEVIDYS contributing $898.7M in its first full commercial year, while the PMOs combined for $965.6M. So, almost a 50/50 split in revenue for ELEVIDYS and the PMOs. However, when we look at the Q4 numbers, the product revenue was $369.6M, with ELEVIDYS contributing $110.4M, which means the PMOs collected $259.2M. So, the company’s revenue is now 30% ELEVIDYS.

Some have proposed that the SRPT sell-off was because analyst expectations had ELEVIDYS bringing in $130M to $150M in revenue for Q4, but they only recorded $110M, and investors were concerned about the “miss” on expectations. However, Sarepta publicized that six infusions were pushed from late December into early January due to the flu season. ELEVIDYS is $3M per infusion, so that is about $18M of deferred revenue going from Q4 of 2025 to Q1 of 2026. I’m not saying that a miss on the Street’s expectations was not a factor in the sell-off, but I think the commercial trends we are seeing from ELEVIDYS are probably a bigger factor.

Below, we have ELEVIDYS' quarterly numbers for 2025.

Q1: ~$375M

Q2: ~$282M

Q3: ~$131.5M

Q4: $110.4M

Clearly, we have to concede that the decrease in revenue is solely due to a drop in demand. We also have to consider that the Q1 to Q2 drop has to do with some normalization after a very strong Q1, which benefited from rapid early uptake. The Q2 to Q3 drop is likely due to the pause on non-ambulatory treatment and ongoing safety concerns. And the drop from Q3 to Q4 was attributed to flu season and safety-related rescheduling, rather than just pure erosion in ELEVIDYS demand.

Regardless of the reasons why, the market is not going to respond positively to a highly touted drug that has seen an astounding first quarter of launch and has only seen those numbers drop off in less than a year.

Is ELEVIDYS A Bust?

Despite the fading sales numbers, I think it is very hard to argue that ELEVIDYS is a bust when it brought in nearly $900M in first-year revenue, which is exceptional for a gene therapy. Yes, we have to revise our expectations for the near term, but Sarepta’s management reiterated a $500M annual revenue floor for ELEVIDYS, and they expect to exceed it. Although the preliminary earnings numbers are concerning, I don’t think it is absurd to expect normalization in the ambulatory population to improve enough to reach or exceed that $500M level.

Yes, we have to accept that the limitation to the ambulatory population has certainly limited peak sales for the time being, but a steady reversal to quarter-over-quarter growth could revitalize the bullish outlook for ELEVIDYS and SRPT.

I think Sarepta could rally investors if ELEVIDYS could hit these quarterly marks.

Q1: $130–150M

Q2: $160–180M

Q3: $180–200M

Q4: $200–230M

I know that I’m proposing a scenario that is well above the company’s estimated floor for ELEVIDY, but I do believe the company is going to easily surpass $500M and show clear upward momentum to get the market to firmly back SRPT. I must highlight that the company claims that around 80% of the ambulatory DMD remains untreated, so the company could still report strong growth with their current label.

Thankfully, the company has not given up on the non-ambulatory population despite the FDA’s revised indication. Sarepta is working on improved safety protocols to treat non-ambulatory patients again. The company has received the green light from the FDA to initiate Cohort 8 of the ENDEAVOR study, which is trying a boosted immunosuppressive treatment for non-ambulatory DMD patients for ELEVIDYS. Approximately 25 participants will receive sirolimus before and after gene therapy infusion to mitigate the risk of acute liver injury. The data would be expected in the second half of this year, but we could see some early safety data before then. Obviously, if the data is positive, we could see the FDA support resuming commercial dosing of ELEVIDYS in the non-ambulatory population towards the end of 2026 or 2027. Now, I must stress that estimates for the percentage of the U.S. DMD population that is ambulatory vs. non-ambulatory are inconsistent, with ranges going from around 1/3 to 1/2. So, getting the non-ambulatory patient population back could roughly double the addressable market in a year.

So, I would say ELEVIDYS is far from being a bust, with a strong first year, a possible resurgence in year two, and an acceleration in year three, which could help put the therapy back on track to a multi-billion dollar peak sales.

Bullish Highlights Beyond ELEVIDYS

While ELEVIDYS is understandably going to determine how investors treat SRPT, I must stress that Sarepta’s fundamentals remain strong. The company’s PMO franchise is still a remarkably underappreciated foundation that is still growing in value, but also in stability. Keep in mind, EXONDYS, VYONDYS, and AMONDYS together brought in nearly $1B in revenue in 2025. So, ELEVIDYS might not experience a huge inflection this year, but investors should feel reassured that the company’s PMOs have what it takes to maintain cash flow until the company unlocks the non-ambulatory population again.

I also need to highlight Sarepta’s financial positioning, which is roughly $954M in cash and investments and no near-term debt. So, I believe the company has the balance sheet and sustained cash flow to fund their siRNA pipeline.

My point is that Sarepta is at the stage where investors should see them as more than a single drug or therapy story. Admittedly, I don’t think the market will see it that way and will take SRPT where the ELEVIDYS outlook is pointing. However, fundamentally, Sarepta is now a self-funding rare-disease platform with a next-gen pipeline and has the cash position to exercise some strategic optionality… with or without ELEVIDYS hitting its estimated peak sales.

Risk Remains

Despite all my points above… I have to reiterate that SRPT is handcuffed to ELEVIDY for the foreseeable future, which will likely attract elevated volatility. Investors must accept that there is a possibility that sales numbers for ELEVIDYS could get worse before they get better. It is possible that the safety concerns will continue to linger, and patients or providers don’t want to risk the therapy. It is also possible that the non-ambulatory study fails, and that population will never be put back on the label. Even if ELEVIDYS gets back to full strength, we have to accept that the company is treating an incredibly fragile patient population, so adverse effects and deaths might be a permanent risk to any of the company’s therapies. Plus, you also have to consider the manufacturing of these gene therapies and the risk that payers become hesitant to continue supporting these therapies with multi-million dollar price tags.

However, in the face of these risks, I’m keeping my SRPT conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio.

My Game Plan

My SRPT approach will largely remain the same but will still demand a high degree of focus, discipline, and flexibility as we move deeper into 2026. I was successful in adding to the position in August, around $15 per share, which made me rethink placing small, incremental buy orders around the $25 level.

Now, I’m going to wait and see if the market wants to bring SRPT back down to $18 per share at some point in January, then $19 in February, and then $20 in March. If so, I will make a small addition for the first time in 2026. On the other hand, if the market starts to push SRPT higher, I will wait for the share price to climb above the anchored-VWAP from the June 2024-high before clicking the buy button.

In the end, the overall strategy remains unchanged: to actively trade SRPT to generate profits while collecting a long-term “house money” position that is funded by the market.

data.title: ADX: 8% Yield On Large Caps
data.description:
Looking for high-yield income from diversified large-cap stocks?

The Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) is one of the oldest closed-end funds, "CEF's," in the US, having begun in 1929. It has been paying dividends since 1929.

Fund Profile:

"Adams Diversified Equity Fund, a closed-end fund, seeks to deliver superior returns over time by investing in a broadly diversified equity portfolio. The fund invests in a blend of high-quality, large-cap companies. The fund seeks to generate returns that exceed its benchmark as well as consistently distribute dividend income and capital gains to shareholders." (ADX site)

ADX is in the top 3 largest market cap equity CEFs, with $3.04B in Net Assets. It holds 94 positions, with an average daily volume of 302K, and no leverage.

Its annual expense ratio is just 0.50%, with no leverage.

Holdings:

Tech is by far the largest sector exposure, at 34.5%, followed distantly by Financials, 13%; Consumer Discretionary, at 10.7%; Communications Services, at 10.1%; Healthcare, at 9%; and Industrials, at 7.8%; and Consumer Staples, at 5%. The remaining sector exposures run from 1.6% to 3.2%:

As of 9/30/25, ADX's top 10 holdings included all the Magnificent 7 stocks, in addition to Broadcom, Chase, and Adams Natural Resources Fund, which gave ADX exposure to energy stocks. The top 10 positions accounted for ~43% of ADX's portfolio.

Dividends:

At its 1/14/26 closing price of $23.10, ADX yielded 7.97% - it pays a quarterly $0.46 distribution.

It has a very high 5-year distribution growth rate of 42.61%, due to its 2021 payouts jumping 189%, from $1.03 to $2.98, and its 2024 payouts rising 92%, from $1.30 to $2.50.

For the 6 months ended 6/30/25, annualized NII rose ~10% vs. full year 2024, whereas annualized Net Realized Gains fell 36%. NII/Gains Distribution coverage was .96X for Q1-2 '25, vs. 1.12X in 2024.

Annualized Unrealized Appreciation for the 6 months ended 6/30/26 was down 19.6%.

Net Assets rose $112M in full year 2024 and $142M in the 6 months ended 6/30/26:

Taxes:

Management estimated that 2025 distributions were comprised of 85% Long Term Gains, 9% NII, and 6% in Short Term Gains:

Performance:

Alphabet (GOOG), Broadcom (AVGO), NVIDIA (NVDA), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) led ADX's top 10 holdings over the past year, with price gains of ~75%, ~51%, 37.5%, and 26%, respectively.

ADX gained 13.3% in price, with a total return of ~21%, outperforming the S&P 500's 19.79% total return. AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, AMZN, META, and PEO were the top 10's lowest performers over the past year.

As for the start of 2026, Alphabet continues to outperform, rising ~7%, and PEO is up 7.2%, whereas the rest of ADX's top 10 are in the red, with ADX itself down ~1%, vs. the S&P's 1.2% gain:

Longer term, ADX has 3-, 5-, and 10-year NAV returns of 23%, 15%, and 15.5%, respectively, with somewhat higher price returns of 28.3%, 17%, and 16.6%:

ADX outperformed the Morningstar US CEF US Equity category on a price and NAV return basis in 2023-2025, 2018-2020, and in 2021 on an NAV basis:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: As seen in the above table, ADX can be negatively impacted by rising interest rates - it fell nearly 20% in 2022, a year of rising rates.

Valuations:

CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion.

ADX's NAV valuation of -6.86% is not nearly as deep of a discount as its 1-, 3-, and 5-year valuations of -8.56%, -11.79%, and -12.81%.

Parting Thoughts:

While ADX has a strong record of outperformance, its current discount is not that attractive. We advise putting it on your watchlist and waiting for a deeper discount before adding new shares.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: The Setup Looks Easy, But It Isn't (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Kinder Morgan's Earnings Approach
